preFix.title: Making a basic piped cushion cover
preFix.subtitle: Sew a tailored cushion cover with a piped seam and zip fastening
preFix.excerpt: How to make a piped cushion cover: cutting the fabric panels, making and joining the piping strip, sewing the panels together, fitting a zip in the back seam, and turning through for a neat finish.
preFix.sourceNotes: Barnett, The Upholsterer's Step-by-Step Handbook. Colefax and Fowler workshop notes.

content.text: A piped cushion cover is the standard upholstery finish for box and scatter cushions. The
piping cord
inserted into the seam gives the cover its crisp edge. The
bias strip
covering the cord is the detail that determines whether the corners are smooth or buckled. This job requires a sewing machine fitted with a piping foot (also called a cording foot or zipper foot).
When to use it
Making cushion covers for sofas, armchairs, and window seats.
Recovering existing cushion pads where the old cover is worn.
Matching existing piped cushions when adding a new piece to a set.
Step-by-step
Step 1, cut the panels.

Cut the top and bottom panels to the cushion pad dimensions plus 15 mm seam allowance on all four sides. Use the upholstery shears and cut against a straight edge. Mark the grain direction on each panel so they are assembled with the grain running the same way.
Step 2, make the piping.

Cut bias strips 40 mm wide from the cover fabric. Join strips end-to-end at 45 degrees to make a continuous length equal to the cushion perimeter plus 100 mm. Fold the bias strip in half over the piping cord and machine-stitch along the cord using the piping foot, keeping the stitch line as close to the cord as possible.
Step 3, sew the piping to the top panel.

Starting at the middle of a side edge (not at a corner), pin and machine-stitch the piping to the right side of the top panel with the raw edges aligned, using the piping foot to stitch close to the cord. Clip the piping seam allowance at each corner to allow the piping to turn at 90 degrees without puckering. Overlap and join the piping ends neatly on the starting side.
Step 4, join the panels and fit the zip.

Insert a zip into the back seam of the bottom panel before assembling. Place the top and bottom panels right sides together with the piping sandwiched inside. Machine-stitch around three sides, then turn through the zip opening. Check all corners with the upholstery regulator to push the seam allowance into the corners cleanly. Insert the pad, close the zip.
Common mistakes
Piping not clipped at corners.
Unclipped piping buckles at each corner. Clip the piping seam allowance to within 2 mm of the stitching at each corner before sewing the second panel.
